source_limitations: Analysis limited to single-feature deterministic decision stump; multifeature interactions not assessed.
Local evidence only; global aggregation or cross-feature validation not performed.
local label inconsistency limits representational stability conclusions

findings: Destination Port feature exhibits strong distributional skew with a dominant port value in DDoS attack instances.
High Jensen-Shannon divergence and low entropy indicate consistent and stable skew patterns across attack instances.
Clear class separation in destination port distribution supports the hypothesis of targeted attack behavior.
Destination Port feature shows strong class-conditioned dependency concentration consistent with attack targeting patterns.
Local label consistency verification revealed high label neighborhood conflicts and duplicate label conflicts, indicating local label inconsistency around Destination Port.
These conflicts suggest epistemic instability in local label topology despite global distributional skew.
limitations: local label inconsistency limits representational stability conclusions

findings: Destination Port feature exhibits strong class-dependent distributional skew with moderate shortcut-like predictive leverage.
This suggests a moderate risk of shortcut exploitation based on destination port distribution differences in this dataset.
limitations: Analysis limited to single-feature deterministic decision stump; multifeature interactions not assessed.
Local evidence only; global aggregation or cross-feature validation not performed.

findings: Destination Port distributions show stable, low-entropy, dominant-value patterns with high class separation across temporal and batch-specific subsets.
No significant temporal or batch-specific variability detected in destination port distribution patterns.
Observed distributional skew supports the hypothesis of targeted attack behavior reflected in destination port usage.
